Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 67 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Orthopaedic Surgery.

67articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

S1P Modulation via S1P Lyase Inhibition Enhances Bone Regeneration in a Murine Critical-size Defect Model of Atrophic Nonunion

Reinkemeier F, Korte R, Bakri A et al. · 2026 Apr 08
Study Type: Experimental animal study (murine model)
Key Question: Does systemic S1P lyase inhibition enhance bone regeneration in a critical-size bone defect model of atrophic nonunion?
Key Findings:
  • DOP treatment significantly increased histologic bone formation (80,000 vs 52,000 pixels, p=0.03) and micro-CT bone volume (20.6% vs 8.8%, p=0.004) compared to controls
  • Enhanced osteoblast differentiation markers (RUNX2 and osteocalcin both p<0.01) and increased osteoclast activity, indicating active bone remodeling
  • Improved cellular proliferation and angiogenesis (PECAM-1 expression 23,000 vs 13,700, p=0.009)
Clinical Relevance: This preclinical work identifies S1P modulation as a potential non-invasive adjunctive therapy for managing atrophic nonunions, which currently have limited treatment options beyond revision surgery in NHS practice.
Limitations: Murine model findings require validation in large animal studies and clinical trials to establish optimal dosing, duration, and safety profile in humans.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

Program Size and City Characteristics May Be Associated With Gender Diversity in Orthopaedic Surgery Residency Programs

Park YL, Grossman M, Collier C et al. · 2026 Apr 08
Study Type: Cross-sectional analysis
Key Question: Are geographic location, program characteristics, and city-level factors associated with gender diversity in orthopaedic surgery residency programs?
Key Findings:
  • No significant differences in women resident percentages across nine US geographic regions defined by ERAS
  • Larger programs were modestly associated with higher percentages of women residents (β 0.28, 95% CI 0.07-0.49, p=0.01)
  • Community programs had 5% fewer women residents compared to university programs (95% CI -8% to -2%, p<0.001)
Clinical Relevance: This analysis provides actionable data for UK orthopaedic training programs seeking to improve gender diversity, suggesting program size and academic affiliation may influence recruitment success.
Limitations: Cross-sectional design cannot establish causality between program characteristics and gender representation.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

Bilateral Open Adductor Longus Tenotomy Is Associated With Predictable Return to Play in Professional Soccer Players: A Case Series of 100 Patients

Cugat R, Montaña F, Barastegui D et al. · 2026 Apr 10
Study Type: Retrospective case series
Key Question: What are the return-to-play outcomes and complications following bilateral open adductor longus tenotomy in professional soccer players with dynamic pubic osteopathy?
Key Findings:
  • All 100 professional soccer players returned to competitive play at median 87 days (IQR 62-128 days)
  • 98% (98/100) resumed competition at their preinjury level
  • No major complications occurred; 2% (2/100) required revision surgery
Clinical Relevance: This provides benchmarking data for counselling elite athletes about expected recovery timelines following this specialised procedure, though dynamic pubic osteopathy is rarely encountered in typical NHS orthopaedic practice.
Limitations: Retrospective design from a single specialist centre limits generalisability to broader surgical practice and patient populations.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

Can a Targeted Dissemination Strategy of the PREPARE Trial Results Change Surgical Antiseptic Practice?

Nesbit SE, Hong I, O'Hara NN et al. · 2026 Apr 10
Study Type: Retrospective analysis
Key Question: Can targeted dissemination strategies accelerate adoption of PREPARE trial findings (favouring iodine povacrylex over chlorhexidine) in orthopaedic fracture surgery?
Key Findings:
  • Iodine povacrylex use increased from 0.8% to 86% immediately after multifaceted dissemination intervention (OR 765, 95% CI 488-1197; p<0.001)
  • High adoption rates sustained over 17 months without additional intervention (no significant trend decline, p=0.20)
  • Fellowship-trained trauma surgeons showed higher adoption (94.8% increase) versus non-fellowship trained (34.7% increase)
Clinical Relevance: Demonstrates that simple, low-cost implementation strategies (education, reminders, supply chain support) can rapidly translate RCT evidence into routine practice, potentially improving surgical site infection rates in UK orthopaedic trauma units.
Limitations: Single-centre study limits generalisability to different healthcare systems and organisational contexts.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

Most Contemporary TKA Implant Systems Meet International Benchmark Survivorship Standards: Evidence From a US Registry

Son SJ, Sambare TD, Sun H et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Which TKA implant systems used in the US meet international benchmark standards for revision-free survivorship at 2, 5, 10, and 15 years?
Key Findings:
  • Of 45 TKA systems evaluated from 288,584 primary TKAs, 93% (42/45) met 2-year benchmarks and 88% (37/42) met 5-year benchmarks
  • At 10 years, 68% (17/25) of systems received benchmarks (52% superior, 16% non-inferior), with superior systems used in 91% of eligible cases
  • Smith & Nephew Journey systems and Zimmer Biomet Natural Knee systems consistently failed to meet benchmarks across multiple timepoints
Clinical Relevance: This large US registry analysis provides evidence-based guidance for UK orthopaedic surgeons selecting TKA implants, identifying specific systems with suboptimal revision rates that may warrant reconsideration in clinical practice.
Limitations: Single healthcare system data may limit generalisability to NHS populations with different demographics and surgical practices.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

Medial Meniscus Posterior Root Shows More Severe Histologic Degeneration in Female Patients With Advanced Osteoarthritis and Varus Alignment: A Histopathologic Analysis of Human Menisci

Dzidzishvili L, Terzi MÁ, Nájar EE et al. · 2026 Apr 23
Study Type: Histopathologic analysis (cross-sectional study)
Key Question: Does osteoarthritis cause selective histological degeneration of the medial meniscus posterior root, particularly in female patients and those with varus alignment?
Key Findings:
  • Medial meniscus posterior roots showed significantly worse histological degeneration than lateral roots (modified Pauli score 6.6±2.6 vs 2.7±1.6, p<0.001)
  • Female sex was independently associated with more severe root degeneration (β = -2.63 for male sex, 95% CI -3.90 to -1.36)
  • Greater varus alignment correlated moderately with worse medial root degeneration (r = 0.54, p<0.001)
Clinical Relevance: This histological evidence helps explain why medial meniscus posterior root tears are more common in female patients with osteoarthritis and varus alignment, potentially informing UK orthopaedic surgeons' risk stratification and surgical planning for total knee arthroplasty patients.
Limitations: Single-centre study with tissue samples only from patients requiring total knee arthroplasty, limiting generalisability to earlier stages of osteoarthritis.
PubMed →DOI →
Clinical orthopaedics and related research

Isolated Proximal Femoral Valgus Osteotomy for Hinge Abduction Improves Femoroacetabular Relationship in Patients With Perthes Disease

Rhee I, Park BK, Shah M et al. · 2026 Apr 24
Study Type: Retrospective case series
Key Question: Does isolated proximal femoral valgus osteotomy improve femoroacetabular relationship and outcomes in Perthes disease patients with hinge abduction deformity?
Key Findings:
  • Femoral head sphericity significantly improved (sphericity deviation score: 62±19 to 34±22, mean difference 28, 95% CI 16-41, p<0.001)
  • Acetabular development improved with progressive increase in centre-edge angle and decreased Sharp angle
  • Pain scores improved dramatically (VAS 6-10 preoperatively to 0-2 at final follow-up) with maintained hip ROM
Clinical Relevance: This challenges the view of valgus osteotomy as purely salvage surgery, suggesting it may be a joint preservation procedure that promotes both femoral and acetabular remodelling in Perthes disease with hinge abduction.
Limitations: Level IV evidence from a single-centre retrospective series without control group comparison.
PubMed →DOI →
The American journal of sports medicine

Isolated Medial Patellofemoral Ligament Reconstruction for Recurrent Patellar Dislocations With Increased Femoral Anteversion and an Absent/Mild J-sign

Zhang Z, Wang D, Liu Y et al. · 2026 Apr 19
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Can isolated MPFL reconstruction achieve good outcomes in patients with recurrent patellar dislocations who have increased femoral anteversion (>30°) but minimal patellar maltracking?
Key Findings:
  • Zero redislocations occurred in 52 patients at mean 2.4-year follow-up after isolated MPFL reconstruction
  • Functional scores improved significantly: Kujala from 58 to 88 points, Lysholm from 62 to 90 points
  • Patients with severe trochlear dysplasia had comparable functional outcomes but greater residual patellar tilt (18° vs 13°)
Clinical Relevance: This challenges the increasing trend toward combined MPFL reconstruction with femoral derotational osteotomy, suggesting isolated MPFL reconstruction may suffice when severe patellar maltracking is absent, potentially avoiding more invasive procedures in selected NHS patients.
Limitations: Short-term follow-up of 2.4 years may not capture late recurrence or degenerative changes from residual anatomical abnormalities.
PubMed →DOI →
